items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: Which mechanism allows motion in one direction only?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ratchet and pawl. “Bell crank” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Rotary to reciprocating. “Linear to rotary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A crank 50 mm long gives a stroke of how many mm in a slider?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
